patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 19-year-old college student working part-time retail. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying you keep getting “nasty” skin infections and chest infections over and over. Volunteer: painful red bumps that become boils and sometimes drain pus; bad cough/chest congestion again this month; on-and-off fevers with flares; feeling worn out most days.
If asked, share: infections recur in same areas even after antibiotics; multiple pneumonias and once needed IV antibiotics; frequent ear/sinus infections since childhood; cuts get infected easily; dry itchy skin with eczema-like patches; mouth sores/white patches after illness; unintentional mild weight loss.
Do not mention unless specifically asked: months of loose stools, getting short of breath on stairs, late tooth eruption/many cavities, easy bruising/tiny red dots after coughing, swollen neck glands.
Resist bringing up genital sores and avoiding close contact due to drainage odor—only admit if directly asked, with embarrassment.
Express misconceptions: you thought it was allergies, asthma with colds, or sensitive skin; tried soaps/diets.
If clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and become more willing to disclose. Ask about costs; worry follow-ups, prophylaxis, genetic/immunology testing are unaffordable; admit stretching meds to make them last.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is that the agent correctly records congenital dyserythropoietic anemia as the working diagnosis to guide counseling and follow-up.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the phrase "congenital dyserythropoietic anemia" and explains it as an inherited disorder of ineffective red blood cell production leading to chronic anemia (and often jaundice/splenomegaly).

Explains expected evaluation at a high level (e.g., CBC with indices, reticulocyte count, hemolysis markers, bilirubin, iron studies; consider bone marrow findings and/or genetic testing) without inventing results or claiming tests were already done.
Includes the communication milestone explain_diagnosis and uses patient-friendly language with a brief check for understanding (e.g., teach-back).

Includes the communication milestone explain_treatment and mentions both "folic acid" and "transfusions" as typical supportive treatments; avoids promising cure and frames treatment as supportive/individualized.
Performs the required safety check allergy_check before any prescribing; if no prescribing occurs, explicitly notes that allergies should be reviewed before starting folic acid or any new medication.

Addresses the noncritical contraindication_check before ordering (e.g., before transfusion planning or iron-related interventions) by stating that contraindications/compatibility factors should be reviewed prior to ordering blood products or other interventions.
Mentions key complications and monitoring needs relevant to CDA (e.g., iron overload from transfusions, gallstones, splenomegaly, growth/development issues) and advises appropriate follow-up.

Provides clear return precautions/red flags (e.g., worsening fatigue, dyspnea, chest pain, syncope, fever/infection signs, dark urine, rapidly increasing jaundice, severe abdominal pain from splenic enlargement).

Avoids unsafe or incorrect management (e.g., does not recommend empiric iron supplementation without evidence of deficiency; does not conflate CDA with autoimmune hemolytic anemia and start steroids; does not suggest emergent transfusion without clinical indication).
Maintains uncertainty appropriately if data are incomplete (e.g., "suspected" vs. "confirmed") and documents a reasonable differential before closing if asked, without overcommitting.

minimal_paths.description: Confirm likely CDA based on chronic anemia since childhood with hemolysis/ineffective erythropoiesis features; counsel and document diagnosis.
must_collect: history of lifelong or childhood-onset anemia
pregnancy status if relevant to anemia management
steps: Elicit key history suggesting inherited chronic anemia and screen for hemolysis symptoms and transfusion history.

Review/ask about prior labs or plan evaluation: CBC with indices, reticulocyte count, peripheral smear; hemolysis labs (bilirubin, LDH, haptoglobin); iron studies; consider B12/folate; consider bone marrow morphology and genetic testing for CDA subtypes.
Provide diagnosis explanation using the term congenital dyserythropoietic anemia and relate symptoms to ineffective RBC production and chronic anemia.
Explain supportive treatment options, explicitly naming folic acid and transfusions, and discuss monitoring for iron overload if transfusions are used.
Document the diagnosis in the chart (WRITE: record_diagnosis).
Create a follow-up plan verbally (even if not using a WRITE tool): hematology follow-up, monitoring labs, and return precautions.

acceptable_variants.description: Probable CDA with explicit uncertainty; plans confirmatory testing and hematology involvement while still recording working diagnosis.
steps: Gather history suggesting inherited anemia and exclude common acquired causes (bleeding, nutritional deficiency, autoimmune hemolysis).
Explain that CDA is suspected; outline confirmatory testing (smear/bone marrow/genetics) and supportive care options including folic acid and transfusions.
Record the working diagnosis as congenital dyserythropoietic anemia (or suspected CDA) and arrange follow-up.

acceptable_variants.description: Includes differential documentation before final diagnosis, then records CDA as most likely.
steps: Elicit history and key features (chronicity, family history, jaundice/splenomegaly, transfusion history).
Discuss differential briefly (e.g., thalassemia, hereditary spherocytosis, G6PD deficiency, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, iron deficiency) and why CDA is favored.
Explain diagnosis and treatment options (folic acid, transfusions) and monitoring for complications.
Record diagnosis as congenital dyserythropoietic anemia.

patient.instructions:
